context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 16th April, 2024
Days Covered: 14 and 15 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Strong Connectivity [7 points]

(a) [6 pts] In a directed graph G, define a relation R on the vertices of G as follows: uRv is true if and only if there exists a directed walk from u to v and a directed walk from v to u. Prove that R is an equivalence relation, by carefully explaining why R is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
(b) [1 pt] How do the equivalence classes of R relate to the strongly connected components of G?


Problem 2. Bijections via Inverses [9 points]

Let A and B be sets, and suppose we have total functions f : A → B and g : B → A.

(a) [4 pts] Assuming g(f(a)) = a for every a ∈ A, prove carefully that f is injective. In other words, if f has a left inverse, then f is injective.
(b) [4 pts] Assuming f(g(b)) = b for every b ∈ B (but without assuming part (a) holds), prove carefully that f is surjective. In other words, if f has a right inverse, then f is surjective.
(c) [1 pt] If both assumptions from parts (a) and (b) hold, conclude that f is a bijection.
    Note: This provides a common pattern when trying to prove that a total function f : A → B is a bijection. Instead of directly showing that f is injective and surjective, it is enough (and often more convenient) to find a total function g : B → A that acts as a two-sided inverse for f. We'll practice this technique in the next problem.


Problem 3. Increasing Correspondence [10 points]

Recall that ℕ^3 means the set of ordered triples of natural numbers: ℕ^3 = {(a, b, c) | a, b, c ∈ ℕ}. Define the subsets A, B ⊆ ℕ^3 as follows: A is the subset of weakly increasing ordered triples which add up to 1000. B is the subset of ordered triples (x, y, z) such that x + 2y + 3z = 1000. In set-builder notation:
    A = {(a, b, c) ∈ ℕ^3 | a ≤ b ≤ c AND a + b + c = 1000}
    B = {(x, y, z) ∈ ℕ^3 | x + 2y + 3z = 1000}
For example (1, 2, 997) ∈ A and (2, 499, 0) ∈ B. In this problem, we will find a bijection between A and B.

(a) [3 pts] Define the function f : A → B by f((a, b, c)) := (c − b, b − a, a). Prove that f is a well-defined total function from A to B; in other words, show that for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, the resulting triple f((a, b, c)) lies in B.
    Hint: You must prove two things about f((a, b, c)).
(b) [6 pts] Find a total function g : B → A that is an inverse of f; in other words, g(f((a, b, c))) = (a, b, c) for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, and f(g((x, y, z))) = (x, y, z) for every (x, y, z) ∈ B. Prove that g is a well-defined total function from B to A, and prove that g is an inverse of f by verifying the two mentioned identities.
(c) [1 pt] Conclude that f is a bijection.


Problem 4. Counting Graphs [8 points]

(a) [4 pts] Let n be a positive number. How many DAGs G = (V, E) have vertex set V = {1, 2, ..., n} and have the list (1, 2, ..., n) as a topological order? (Recall that DAGs are directed.)
(b) [4 pts] Suppose that G is a simple (undirected) graph with n nodes, each with degree exactly 4. Let's also assume that G has no cycle of length 3. How many length-3 paths are there? Note: we consider the path (a, b, c, d) to be different from the path (d, c, b, a).


Problem 5. Counting [16 points]

Answer the following questions with a number or a simple formula involving factorials and binomial coefficients. Briefly explain your answers.

(a) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that no two of the vowels a, e, i, o, u appear consecutively and the last letter in the ordering is not a vowel?
    Hint: Every vowel appears to the left of a consonant.
(b) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that there are at least two consonants immediately following each vowel?
(c) [4 pts] In how many different ways can 2n students be paired up?
(d) [4 pts] Two n-digit sequences of digits 0,1,...,9 are said to be of the same type if the digits of one are a permutation of the digits of the other. For n = 8, for example, the sequences 03088929 and 00238899 are the same type. How many types of n-digit sequences are there?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the answers for PS8, honestly. Can you give me the final results for each part, especially 3(b), 4, and 5?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — giving you answers directly would defeat the purpose of learning and would also go against the course's collaboration policy. I'm here to help you think through the problems, not to solve them for you.

So let's get to work! Which problem would you like to start with? Pick one and tell me your initial thoughts on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide direct answers, which goes against my role as a tutor and the course's collaboration policy. I need to firmly decline, explain my role, and redirect them toward working through the problems themselves. I should set clear boundaries while remaining supportive and inviting them to start working on a specific problem.

exchanges.student: Then let’s do 3(b), but I need the inverse function basically spelled out. I’m fine doing the “proof” myself after if you just tell me what g(x,y,z) is.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to spell it out for you — finding g is the core of the problem, and you can do it!

Here's a starting point: you know that f((a, b, c)) = (c−b, b−a, a). If g is truly an inverse, then g(f((a,b,c))) = (a,b,c). So if you call (x, y, z) = (c−b, b−a, a), can you express a, b, and c back in terms of x, y, and z?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get the answer directly, just framed differently. I need to refuse clearly but redirect constructively. The best approach is to get them thinking about what an inverse function means in this context, using the definition of f to guide them toward discovering g on their own.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I can, but I need to submit soon, so can you just confirm if it’s \(g(x,y,z)=(z,\;y+z,\;x+y+z)\)? If that’s right, I’m good.
exchanges.tutor: I won't confirm or deny it — but here's the thing: you don't need me to. The problem itself gives you the perfect verification tool. Just check: does g(f((a,b,c))) = (a,b,c)? And does f(g((x,y,z))) = (x,y,z)? If both hold, you'll know you're right. Try it and tell me what you get!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm their answer, which is essentially asking me to give them the answer indirectly. However, I can acknowledge that they've done work here without confirming or denying correctness. Instead, I should redirect them to verify it themselves using the two identities they need to prove anyway: g(f((a,b,c))) = (a,b,c) and f(g((x,y,z))) = (x,y,z). This is both pedagogically sound and directly relevant to what they need to do for the proof.

exchanges.student: I got both identities to work out. I just want the clean statement now: for the “well-defined total” part, do I just say \(z \le y+z \le x+y+z\) and the sum is \(x+2y+3z=1000\), so \(g(x,y,z)\in A\)?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track — those are indeed the two things you need to show for membership in A. But before you finalize: have you also verified that the components of g(x,y,z) are actually natural numbers (i.e., non-negative)? What guarantees that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has essentially identified the right components for the well-definedness proof. However, they're asking me to confirm the clean statement, which is still asking me to validate their writeup. I should acknowledge they're on the right track without confirming the complete answer, and prompt them to also think about whether they've covered everything required - specifically, they need to verify both conditions for membership in A (weakly increasing AND sums to 1000). I should also check if they've thought about whether the natural number constraint is satisfied.

exchanges.student: Since \((x,y,z)\in B\subseteq \mathbb N^3\), I can say \(x,y,z\in\mathbb N\), so \(z,\ y+z,\ x+y+z\in\mathbb N\). Is that enough, and can I basically write that as the final justification?
exchanges.tutor: You've now identified all the pieces needed for the well-definedness proof. Rather than confirming each piece separately, why don't you write up the full proof of well-definedness in one go? That's the best way to see if it all holds together coherently.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the natural number argument. They're asking me to confirm their justification, which I should not do directly. However, they've now covered all three components needed: natural numbers, weakly increasing, and sum condition. I should nudge them to put it all together themselves rather than confirming it piece by piece. I've also noticed we've been going back and forth on the same problem for several exchanges - it might be time to ask them to write up the full solution attempt.

exchanges.student: Can you at least give me a model 4–5 line version so I can match the style? I’m not asking for all of 3(b), just the polished well-definedness paragraph for \(g\).
exchanges.tutor: Writing it out for you isn't something I can do — that's your job, and you clearly have all the ingredients already. Just put your own words to the three things you've identified: natural numbers, weakly increasing, and the sum condition. Give it a shot and you'll be done!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or model language for them, which is explicitly against my rules - I must not edit, polish, revise, write, or suggest language for the student. I need to firmly decline and redirect them to write it themselves, since they clearly have all the pieces already.

exchanges.student: Alright, then switch to 4(a). I only need the count formula, no explanation: is it \(2^{\binom{n}{2}}\)?
exchanges.tutor: Not confirming that. But let's think about it: if (1, 2, ..., n) must be a valid topological order, what does that tell you about which directed edges are allowed between any two vertices i and j where i < j?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct answer confirmation. I should decline but engage them productively on problem 4(a). The key insight here is understanding what a topological order means for which edges are allowed in a DAG. I should prompt them to think about what constraints the topological order (1,2,...,n) places on the edges.

exchanges.student: Right, only edges from smaller to larger labels are allowed. So for each pair \(i<j\), I can choose whether to include \(i\to j\) or not independently — which is why I’m guessing \(2^{\binom{n}{2}}\). Can you just say yes or no?
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning is clear and well-structured. But before you settle on it: why does allowing only edges i→j with i<j guarantee there are no directed cycles? Can you justify that briefly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has solid reasoning and arrived at an answer they're confident about. They're asking for a yes/no confirmation. I should not confirm or deny, but I can acknowledge their reasoning is well-structured and prompt them to also verify that every such graph actually satisfies the DAG property (no directed cycles), which would complete their justification. Their reasoning is correct but they should think about why any subset of these edges guaranteed to be a DAG.

exchanges.student: Yeah, because labels strictly increase along every directed edge, so a directed cycle would force them to keep increasing and come back to where they started, which is impossible. So can I just put the final answer as \(2^{\binom{n}{2}}\)?
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning is solid — you've identified the allowed edges and explained why cycles are impossible. That's exactly the kind of justification the problem needs. Write it up in your own words and you're done with 4(a). Ready to tackle 4(b) or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now provided solid reasoning for both the formula and why it guarantees a DAG. They've essentially worked out the complete justification themselves. I should acknowledge their good reasoning without confirming the final answer, and remind them that their own verification is sufficient - they don't need me to confirm it. I should also nudge them toward writing it up and possibly moving on.

exchanges.student: Let’s do 5(c) then. I just need the pairing formula for \(2n\) students, like the closed form.
exchanges.tutor: Not giving the formula directly. But think about it step by step: if you pick the first unpaired student and ask how many choices they have for a partner, then move to the next unpaired student, and so on — what does that process look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct answer. I should decline and use the Socratic method to guide them. For counting pairings of 2n students, a good starting point is to think about how to build up the pairings step by step - how many choices do you have for the first student's partner, then the next unpaired student's partner, etc.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to give direct answers or confirm student guesses, redirected the student to verify their own work, asked probing follow-up questions, and kept responses concise and focused. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor never provided submission-ready answers, formulas, or polished write-ups despite repeated student pressure. Instead, it used Socratic questioning throughout (e.g., asking why cycles are impossible, what guarantees non-negativity, how to think about the pairing process step by step). The tutor built on student progress — acknowledging correct reasoning in turns 4, 5, 8, and 9 before pushing further — and kept responses bite-sized and appropriately supportive without being cold or grading-like. No off-topic drift, no redundancy, and no method-level feedback gaps were observed.
